judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with comparable substance; Report 1 states the checkout-insufficiency nuance for the results-DB collision slightly more explicitly, while Report 2 adds extra scrutiny (F-02 endpoint URL, non-git working copy) not required by the rubric — overall they are roughly equal in quality.

report_1.evidence: Batch B — registry: F-02, F-05, F-09, all three of which are the entirety of pipeline/registry.py. One engineer owns this file; splitting three one-line config fixes across people guarantees conflicts for no gain.

report_2.evidence: Engineer C — registry config (pipeline/registry.py): F-02, F-05, F-09. All three are edits to one 12-line config file; trivially one person.

report_1.evidence: Wave 1 (fully parallel, file-disjoint): ... Batch A — tests: F-11 (rubric edge-case parsing test) and F-12 (pagination boundary test) ... Batch B — registry: F-02, F-05, F-09 ... One engineer owns this file

report_2.evidence: Ordering: A, B, C, D can all start immediately in parallel — their file sets are disjoint. ... Engineer B — rubric parsing (pipeline/rubric.py): F-01 plus its test F-11. ... Engineer C — registry config (pipeline/registry.py): F-02, F-05, F-09. All three are edits to one 12-line config file; trivially one person.

report_1.evidence: F-06 ... This changes scoring outcomes for every graded dataset — it's a product/policy decision, not a bug ... Assigning it means an engineer guesses new thresholds and silently shifts every score. Escalate it for a decision on the actual values (ideally with before/after distributions), without blocking the other batches.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — a policy decision, not a bug fix. ... changing PARTIAL_CREDIT_THRESHOLDS silently shifts every score, breaking comparability with all previously recorded results. This needs an owner to specify the new thresholds and decide on re-baselining ... I'd send F-06 back for concrete acceptance criteria and sequence it last.

report_1.evidence: Shared test infrastructure: tests/conftest.py points every test run at the same /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db. Parallel test runs from multiple checkouts would collide on that database unless each engineer sets their own RESULTS_DB.

report_2.evidence: tests/conftest.py:4 reads RESULTS_DB (default /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db); each engineer should set RESULTS_DB to a private per-engineer path so parallel test runs don't share one database file.
